content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Robert D. Winston 
General Counsel 
october 21, 1988 
Lake Shastina Community Services District 
P.o. Box 669 
Weed, CA 
96094 
Re: 
Our File No. G-88-298 
Dear Mr. Winston: 
You have requested information concerning the 
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(the "Act").Y Specifically, you have requested clarification 
of our advice concerning the rule of "legally required 
participation" under section 87101. 
You have asked if the 
Board of Directors of the Lake Shastina Community Services 
District should use that rule when the board cannot obtain a 
quorum on decisions concerning litigation directly involving a 
majority of the board members, rather than allowing only the 
disinterested directors to vote. 
Enclosed is a copy of the Commission's Hudson op1n1on (In 
re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13) and a copy of Regulation 
18701. 
Both the Hudson opinion and Regulation 18701 permit 
otherwise disqualified public officials to participate in 
governmental decisions when their participation is legally 
necessary, such as when a quorum cannot otherwise be achieved. 
The opinion and regulation assume, however, that a quorum of 
the decisionmaking body is legally required for the proposed 
action. 
Therefore, if a quorum is necessary for the action proposed 
by the Board of Directors of the Lake Shastina Community 
Services District, the rule of "legally required participation" 
Y 
Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code 
of Regulations section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, DiVIsion 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 


Robert D. Winston 
October 21, 1988 
Page 2 
may be used to achieve a quorum when no quorum otherwise may be 
obtained. 
I trust this information is sufficient for your purposes. 
If you have any further questions, please contact me at (916) 
322-5901. 
Sincerely, 
~ _ 
cf(~ t, ~l/U-rz;L-~ 
Kathryn ~novan 
Counsel, Legal Division 


WRA Y E. KlRSHER 
ROBERT D. WINSTON 
ROBERT B. BOSTON 
KIRSHER, WINSTON, & BOSTON 
July 25, 1988 
Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
428 "J" Street, #800 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
RE: Legal Assistance 
Dear Sir or Madame: 
A LAW CORPORATION 
219 Main Street 
P. O. Box 669 
Weed, California 96094 
(916) 938-3438 
Mt. Shasta Office 
326 N. Mt. Shasta Blvd., Suite 9 
P. O. Box 263 
Mt. Shasta, CA 96067 
Due to litigation directly involving a majority of the 
members of the Board of Directors of the Lake Shastina 
Community Services District, we anticipate failing to 
obtain a voting quorum on some resolutions to be pre-
sented to the Board in relation to such litigation. 
Should we allow the disinterested directors vote on 
the resolutions, though they are less than a quorum, 
or instead, randomly select enough disqualified directors 
so as to constitute a quorum, as you advised in your 
File No. A-88-151? 
Thank you for your help. 
Sincerely, 
ert D. Winston 
General Counsel 
Lake Shastina C.S.D. 
RDW/glt 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
File No. T-88-298 
Date 
: 
October 2, 1989 
From 
FAIR POUTICAl PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
Subject : 
Telephone Advice to Robert Winston, Lake Shastina Community 
Service District 
On August 1, 1988, I spoke to Robert Winston, attorne~ for 
the Lake Shastina Community Service District, and advised him that 
when that agency could not obtain a quorum due to disqualified 
directors, it was appropriate to randomly select enough 
disqualified directors so as to constitute a quorum. 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Robert D. Winston 
General Counsel 
Lake Shastina Community 
Services District 
P.O. Box 669 
Weed, CA 
96094 
Dear Mr. Winston: 
July 25, 1988 
Re: 
88-298 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on July 26, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact Kathryn Donovan, an attorney in 
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your reqUest will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • 
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 9,)804~0807 • 
(q 1 A) 17 7 ~C;l\l\n

sections.question: You have asked if the Board of Directors of the Lake Shastina Community Services District should use that rule when the board cannot obtain a quorum on decisions concerning litigation directly involving a majority of the board members, rather than allowing only the disinterested directors to vote.
sections.conclusion: Both the Hudson opinion and Regulation 18701 permit otherwise disqualified public officials to participate in governmental decisions when their participation is legally necessary, such as when a quorum cannot otherwise be achieved. Therefore, if a quorum is necessary for the action proposed by the Board of Directors of the Lake Shastina Community Services District, the rule of "legally required participation" may be used to achieve a quorum when no quorum otherwise may be obtained.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a local agency board invoke the "legally required participation" exemption to allow disqualified directors to participate in voting when litigation involves a majority of board members and disqualified directors are needed to achieve a quorum?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the "legally required participation" rule under section 87101 permits disqualified directors to vote when necessary to achieve a quorum, provided that a quorum is legally required for the proposed action.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple components: initial advice request letter from July 25, 1988; acknowledgment letter from July 25, 1988; internal memorandum from October 2, 1989 documenting prior telephone advice; and formal written advice letter from October 21, 1988. Extracted primary FPPC response (October 21, 1988 letter from Kathryn Donovan). Some inconsistency noted: phone memo indicates August 1 advice differed from written advice, but written response provided is the authoritative formal guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked if the Board of Directors of the Lake Shastina Community Services District should use that rule when the board cannot obtain a quorum on decisions concerning litigation directly involving a majority of the board members, rather than allowing only the disinterested directors to vote.

CONCLUSION: Both the Hudson opinion and Regulation 18701 permit otherwise disqualified public officials to participate in governmental decisions when their participation is legally necessary, such as when a quorum cannot otherwise be achieved. Therefore, if a quorum is necessary for the action proposed by the Board of Directors of the Lake Shastina Community Services District, the rule of "legally required participation" may be used to achieve a quorum when no quorum otherwise may be obtained.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Lake Shastina Community Services District's question about whether disqualified board members may participate in voting when their participation is necessary to achieve a quorum in litigation matters. The FPPC confirmed that the "legally required participation" exception applies when a quorum is legally required.
